section.title: Paul's Longing and the Theme
section.sectionRationale: After the greeting, Paul opens his heart. He thanks God for the Romans' world-famous faith, tells them how constantly he prays for them and longs to visit, and confesses his sense of debt to preach the gospel to all people. Then, in two verses that will govern the next fifteen chapters, he states his theme: he is not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, and in it the righteousness of God is revealed from faith to faith.
section.thesis: The gospel Paul longs to preach is no cause for shame but the very power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, Jew first and also Greek, because in it God's own righteousness is revealed and received by faith from start to finish, so that the righteous live by faith.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Paul's thanksgiving and constant prayer for the Roman believers; his long-frustrated longing to come and his debt to Greek and non-Greek alike; and the thesis of the whole letter, the gospel as the saving power of God that reveals the righteousness of God by faith.

units.label: Thanksgiving and constant prayer
units.note: their faith proclaimed all over the world

verses.text: First, I thank my God through Jesus Christ for all of you, because your faith is being proclaimed all over the world.
God, whom I serve with my spirit in preaching the gospel of His Son, is my witness how constantly I remember you
in my prayers at all times, asking that now at last by God’s will I may succeed in coming to you.
For I long to see you so that I may impart to you some spiritual gift to strengthen you,
that is, that you and I may be mutually encouraged by each other’s faith.

units.label: The apostle's debt to all
units.note: obligated to Greeks and non-Greeks, wise and foolish

verses.text: I do not want you to be unaware, brothers, how often I planned to come to you (but have been prevented from visiting until now), in order that I might have a harvest among you, just as I have had among the other Gentiles.
I am obligated both to Greeks and non-Greeks, both to the wise and the foolish.
That is why I am so eager to preach the gospel also to you who are in Rome.

units.label: Not ashamed: the power of God for salvation
units.note: the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith

verses.text: I am not ashamed of the {{euangelion|gospel}}, because it is the {{dynamis|power}} of God for {{soteria|salvation}} to everyone who believes, first to the Jew, then to the Greek.
For the gospel reveals the {{dikaiosyne|righteousness}} of God that comes by {{pistis|faith}} from start to finish, just as it is written: “The righteous will live by faith.”

euangelion.gloss: Good news, glad tidings, the announcement of a victory or a royal accession. For Paul the gospel is the message of what God has done in the death and resurrection of His Son. It is the one thing he is set apart to preach, and here he declares that far from being ashamed of it, he glories in it as the very instrument through which God saves.

dynamis.gloss: Power, might, effective force; the root of the English word dynamite. The gospel is not merely information about salvation but the actual power of God unleashed to accomplish it. What the law could not do and human effort cannot achieve, the announced word of the cross effects, creating faith and rescuing all who believe.

soteria.gloss: Salvation, deliverance, rescue, and the wholeness that follows. It embraces rescue from wrath, sin, and death, and the restoration of the whole person to God. In Romans it stretches from justification now to final glory, a deliverance offered to everyone who believes, without distinction of Jew or Greek.

dikaiosyne.gloss: Righteousness, right standing, conformity to what is just. The righteousness of God is both His own saving justice and the righteous status He gives to those who believe. Revealed in the gospel, it is not achieved by human works but received as a gift, the pivot on which the whole letter turns.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, reliance; a confident resting in God and His promise. Righteousness comes from faith to faith, by faith from first to last, so that faith is the empty hand that receives what God gives in Christ. The quotation from Habakkuk seals it: the righteous will live by faith.

basic.context: Having greeted the church, Paul now does what ancient letters did next: he offers thanksgiving and speaks of his prayers. But he turns even this into gospel. He thanks God for the Romans' faith, explains why he has not yet come though he has longed to, and confesses his obligation to preach to everyone. Then he states the theme of the whole letter in verses 16 and 17.
basic.hermeneutics: Read verses 8 to 15 as warm, personal groundwork that leads to the great thesis of 16 and 17. The thesis is the hinge of the letter: everything before it prepares for it, and everything after it unpacks it. The little word for at the start of verses 16 and 17 links them in a chain of reasons, ending in the Scripture quotation that clinches the argument.
basic.originalLanguages: The heart of the passage is a cluster of key terms. The euangelion is the good news; it is the dynamis, the power, of God for soteria, salvation; and in it the dikaiosyne, the righteousness, of God is revealed and received by pistis, faith, from faith to faith.
basic.typology: Habakkuk's word to a waiting, threatened people, the righteous will live by faith, finds its fullest meaning here. What sustained the faithful under judgment becomes the charter of the gospel age. The prophet's promise anticipates the justification of all who believe, Jew first and also Greek.
basic.christ: Paul thanks God through Jesus Christ and serves in preaching the gospel of His Son. The gospel that is God's saving power is the message of Christ crucified and risen. The righteousness of God revealed in the gospel is the righteousness secured by Christ and credited to those united to Him by faith.
basic.god: God is the one Paul thanks, the one he serves, the one whose will governs his travels. The gospel is the power of God, and the righteousness revealed is the righteousness of God. Salvation is God's work from beginning to end, offered to everyone who believes.
basic.crossReferences: Habakkuk 2:4 supplies the anchoring quotation. 1 Corinthians 1:18 calls the message of the cross the power of God. Romans 3:21-22 unfolds the righteousness of God now revealed apart from law. Romans 10:9-13 shows salvation for everyone who calls on the Lord, Jew and Greek alike.
basic.oneStory: The long story of Scripture reaches its turning point. The righteousness God required and humanity could never produce is now revealed as a gift in the gospel. Habakkuk's ancient promise that the righteous will live by faith becomes the door through which people of every nation enter, so that the saving power of God goes out to the whole world.

context.literary: Verses 8 to 15 form the letter's thanksgiving and travelogue, a standard section of Paul's letters, expressing affection and explaining his plans. They build toward the thesis statement of verses 16 and 17, arguably the most important two verses in the letter, which announce the theme that the following chapters expound: the gospel as the saving power of God that reveals the righteousness of God by faith.
context.historical: Writing around AD 57, probably from Corinth, Paul explains to a church he did not found why he has so long delayed coming. The Roman believers' faith was known across the empire. Paul intends Rome as a base for a mission to Spain, and this letter prepares the way by setting out the gospel he preaches and hopes to share with them in person.
context.canonical: Romans 1:16-17 stands at the head of the doctrinal argument of the New Testament's fullest exposition of the gospel. Its citation of Habakkuk 2:4 binds the two Testaments, and the same text echoes in Galatians 3:11 and Hebrews 10:38. Historically these verses lit the Reformation, as Luther came to see the righteousness of God as a gift received by faith rather than a standard that condemns.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure the Roman church of his genuine love and prayers, to explain his repeated but frustrated desire to visit, and above all to announce the theme of his letter: that he is unashamed of the gospel because it is God's saving power, revealing a righteousness from God that is received by faith from first to last.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The thanksgiving and travel plans describe Paul's particular situation. The thesis of verses 16 and 17 is prescriptive and permanent: the gospel is always the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, and righteousness is always received by faith, in every age and for every people.

debates.question: What does the righteousness of God mean in verse 17?

views.label: A gift of right standing from God
views.case: The Reformation reading takes it as the righteous status God graciously gives and credits to believers in Christ, received by faith apart from works; this fits the flow into chapters 3 and 4 and the Habakkuk citation.

views.label: God's own saving activity or covenant faithfulness
views.case: Others stress it as God's own righteousness in acting to save and keep His promises; rightly held, this is not opposed to the gift but names the God who justifies, though it must not eclipse the imputed standing given to the believer.

debates.bearing: The historic evangelical reading safeguards the good news that guilty sinners are declared righteous as a gift received by faith; God's saving faithfulness and the gift of right standing meet in the justification of the ungodly.

debates.question: What does from faith to faith, or by faith from start to finish, mean?

views.label: Faith from first to last
views.case: The phrase stresses that the whole of salvation, its beginning and its continuance, is by faith and nothing else; righteousness is received by faith and lived out by faith.

views.label: From God's faithfulness to human faith
views.case: Some read it as moving from God's faithfulness to the answering faith of the believer; this captures a true dynamic but the emphasis in context falls on faith as the sole means of receiving righteousness.

debates.bearing: Either way the point stands: faith, not works, is the means from beginning to end, which is exactly why Paul can quote Habakkuk that the righteous will live by faith.

study.originalLanguages: The thesis gathers up the vocabulary that will drive the letter. Paul is unashamed of the euangelion, the good news of Christ, because it is the dynamis, the effective power, of God unto soteria, a full and final salvation, for everyone who believes. In this gospel the dikaiosyne, the righteousness of God, both His saving justice and the standing He gives, is revealed and granted by pistis, faith, from faith to faith. The Habakkuk citation crowns the terms: the one who is righteous, righteous by faith, will live.

typology.type: The righteous who live by faith
typology.fulfillment: Habakkuk's word to a people awaiting judgment is fulfilled in the gospel age, where all who believe, Jew and Greek, are justified and given life by faith in Christ, Habakkuk 2:4.

typology.type: The Jew first and also the Greek
typology.fulfillment: The ancient pattern of salvation coming to Israel and then to the nations is fulfilled as the gospel goes out from Israel's Messiah to everyone who believes, without distinction.

typology.type: The power of God to save
typology.fulfillment: The saving might God displayed in the exodus and the return from exile is surpassed in the gospel, the power of God for a salvation that reaches the whole person and the whole world.

study.christ: Though Christ is not named in every line, He is everywhere present. Paul thanks God through Jesus Christ and serves in preaching the gospel of God's Son. The gospel that is the power of God is the message of the crucified and risen Christ. The righteousness of God revealed and given is the righteousness won by Christ and reckoned to all who trust Him. To live by faith is to live by faith in Him, the one in whom Habakkuk's promise comes true.
study.god: The passage is saturated with God. Paul thanks his God, serves God with his spirit, submits his plans to God's will, and preaches a gospel that is the power of God revealing the righteousness of God. Salvation is God's own initiative and accomplishment, offered freely to everyone who believes. The God who kept His word to Habakkuk now reveals His righteousness and saves a people from every nation by faith.

crossReferences.note: The prophetic word Paul quotes: the righteous will live by faith, the seed of the whole doctrine of justification.
The message of the cross is the power of God to those being saved, echoing the gospel as the power of God here.
The righteousness of God now revealed apart from law, through faith in Jesus Christ, for all who believe.
No distinction between Jew and Greek; everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved, unfolding first to the Jew, then to the Greek.

study.oneStory: Scripture had promised a righteousness that God Himself would provide and a salvation that would reach the nations. Here that promise breaks into the open. The gospel of God's Son is the power of God to save everyone who believes, and in it the righteousness of God is revealed and given by faith. Habakkuk's word to a waiting people becomes the charter of the church: the righteous will live by faith, Jew first and also Greek, until the whole world hears.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say he is not ashamed of the gospel?

options.text: Because it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes.
options.note: This is Paul's own stated reason in verse 16, the thesis of the letter.

options.text: Because in it the righteousness of God is revealed and received by faith.
options.note: True and closely related: verse 17 gives this as a further reason, though verse 16's stated ground is that the gospel is God's saving power.

options.text: Because it is a wise philosophy that impresses the educated.
options.note: Paul preaches to wise and foolish alike, but the gospel's worth is its saving power, not its cleverness.

options.text: Because Rome respected new religions.
options.note: Paul's confidence is in God's power in the gospel, not in Roman tolerance.

quiz.q: What is revealed in the gospel according to verse 17?

options.text: The righteousness of God, received by faith from first to last.
options.note: Paul says the gospel reveals the righteousness of God that comes by faith, from faith to faith.

options.text: God's own saving activity and covenant faithfulness.
options.note: This names a real dimension of the righteousness of God, but must not eclipse the gift of right standing received by faith.

options.text: A new law to be obeyed for acceptance.
options.note: The gospel reveals a righteousness received by faith, not a law that justifies by works.

options.text: Hidden knowledge available only to the elite.
options.note: It is openly revealed to everyone who believes, not secret knowledge for a few.

quiz.q: What Old Testament text does Paul quote to seal his theme?

options.text: Habakkuk 2:4, the righteous will live by faith.
options.note: Paul cites Habakkuk to ground justification by faith in Scripture.

options.text: Genesis 15:6, Abraham believed God, quoted later in chapter 4.
options.note: Closely related, and Paul does use it for justification by faith in chapter 4, but here the quotation is from Habakkuk 2:4.

options.text: Leviticus 18:5, do this and live.
options.note: That text speaks of life by doing; Paul instead quotes Habakkuk on life by faith.

options.text: Deuteronomy 6:4, the Lord is one.
options.note: The Shema is not in view here; Paul quotes Habakkuk 2:4.

quiz.q: To whom does the gospel bring salvation, and in what order?

options.text: To everyone who believes, first to the Jew, then to the Greek.
options.note: Paul states salvation is for all who believe, with a historical priority for the Jew.

options.text: Only to Jews, since salvation belongs to Israel alone.
options.note: This rightly catches the priority first to the Jew, but the gospel is expressly for the Greek too, for everyone who believes.

options.text: Only to Gentiles, since Israel had rejected the gospel.
options.note: The gospel is for the Greek, but Paul insists it is first to the Jew, not Gentiles only.

options.text: To everyone automatically, whether they believe or not.
options.note: Salvation is for everyone who believes; faith is the necessary means.

quiz.q: Why had Paul not yet come to Rome?

options.text: He had often planned to come but had been prevented until now.
options.note: Paul says he planned to come many times but was hindered until now.

options.text: He had been occupied completing his missionary work in the eastern regions.
options.note: Essentially right: Romans 15 says his work in the east had kept him, which is what he means by being prevented until now.

options.text: He was waiting until the church there grew larger.
options.note: He wanted a harvest among them, but the church's size was not the reason for his delay.

options.text: He was ashamed to preach in the capital.
options.note: He explicitly says he is eager to preach the gospel in Rome and is not ashamed of it.

questions: Paul thanks God for the Romans' faith, which was proclaimed all over the world. Whose faith has strengthened yours from a distance, and for whom might God be calling you to give thanks?

Paul longed to give and to receive, to be mutually encouraged by their shared faith. How does it change your view of Christian fellowship to know that even an apostle expected to be encouraged by other believers?
Paul felt himself a debtor to preach the gospel to all kinds of people. Who are the Greeks and non-Greeks, the wise and the foolish, that God has placed within your reach?
Paul says he is not ashamed of the gospel. Where are you tempted to be ashamed of it, and how does remembering that it is the power of God for salvation steady you?
The righteous will live by faith, from first to last. In what area of life are you tempted to live by your own performance rather than resting in what God has given by faith?
The gospel is for everyone who believes, without distinction. How should the fact that God saves Jew and Greek alike shape the way you regard people unlike yourself?

translationNotes.note: The phrase rendered that comes by faith from start to finish translates a Greek expression, from faith to faith, which the footnote gives as for in it is revealed the righteousness of God from faith to faith. The sense is that righteousness is received by faith from beginning to end.

The quotation the righteous will live by faith is from Habakkuk 2:4. The same text is cited in Galatians 3:11 and Hebrews 10:38, and became a watchword of the Reformation's recovery of justification by faith.
Greeks and non-Greeks renders a phrase that is literally to Greeks and barbarians, meaning the whole of humanity, both the cultured and those outside Greek culture; Paul owes the gospel to all alike.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 1:8-17. Footnote markers and inline note objects were skipped when building verse text; line breaks were treated as spaces. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
